title: Print Matrix In Spiral Fashion

content.problemStatement:
Given an `N x M` integer matrix, print its element in spiral fashion (clockwise).

See the following example:



Output should be: 1 2 3 4 8 12 11 10 9 5 6 7


Input Format

The first line of input will contain two space separated integers NNN and MMM, denoting the no. of rows and columns in the input matrix.
Next NNN lines contains MMM space separated integers, the elements of the matrix.


Output Format

Output on N∗MN*MN∗M space separated integers, the elements of input matrix in spiral fashion.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with two integers N and M denoting the number of rows and columns of the matrix. Then N lines follow, each containing M space‑separated integers representing the matrix rows.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the elements of the matrix in clockwise spiral order, separated by a single space. Print a newline after each test case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The matrix is traversed clockwise starting from the top‑left corner, yielding the sequence 1 2 3 4 8 12 11 10 9 5 6 7.
Spiral order of a 2×2 matrix is top row, right column, bottom row, left column.
With a single row, the spiral order is just the row itself.

solution.approach: We keep four boundaries – top, bottom, left and right – that denote the current outer rectangle of the matrix that has not been visited yet. In each iteration we traverse the top row from left to right, then the rightmost column from top to bottom, then the bottom row from right to left (if still inside), and finally the leftmost column from bottom to top (if still inside). After each side is processed we shrink the corresponding boundary. The process stops when the top boundary passes the bottom or the left passes the right. This visits every cell exactly once, giving the required clockwise spiral order.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N·M) per test case, because each matrix element is visited once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N·M) for storing the matrix; the algorithm itself uses O(1) extra space.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- >0){StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int n=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int m=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int[][] a=new int[n][m];for(int i=0;i<n;i++){st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());for(int j=0;j<m;j++){a[i][j]=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());}}int top=0,bottom=n-1,left=0,right=m-1;while(top<=bottom && left<=right){for(int j=left;j<=right;j++)sb.append(a[top][j]).append(' ');top++;for(int i=top;i<=bottom;i++)sb.append(a[i][right]).append(' ');right--;if(top<=bottom){for(int j=right;j>=left;j--)sb.append(a[bottom][j]).append(' ');bottom--;}if(left<=right){for(int i=bottom;i>=top;i--)sb.append(a[i][left]).append(' ');left++;}}sb.append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
